main_turf.bias_notes: Clockwise. A country circuit set alongside the Namoi River. Its absolute defining characteristic is a notoriously short 200m home straight. Because the run home is so incredibly brief, the track plays with a massive on-pace and front-runner bias. Deep closers ('Swoopers') are at a severe mathematical disadvantage unless the early pace completely collapses.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside barriers (1-4) hold a massive advantage, particularly in the 1000m and 1250m sprint dashes. Horses drawn wide must expend significant early energy to cross over or risk being caught wide on the turns, which is heavily penalizing given the lack of a straight to recover in.

handicapping_angles.the_bullring_trap: Aggressively downgrade deep closers ('S' types). With only 200 metres of straight runway, horses settling in the back half of the field will almost always run out of real estate, regardless of their closing splits. Heavily upgrade pure early speed ('E' types).
handicapping_angles.the_tamworth_spillover: Gunnedah sits in the North-West racing region, meaning it frequently shares horse populations with Tamworth and Armidale. However, Tamworth features a 360m straight; horses that rely on a long wind-up at Tamworth will often fail to navigate the sharp turn and short straight here.
handicapping_angles.regional_class_penalty: This is deep regional country form. If you are evaluating a horse whose past performances are built primarily at Gunnedah, you must apply the maximum class penalty if they are shipping down to Provincial tracks (like Newcastle or Hawkesbury) or Metro venues.
